title: Package Python TUI client and developer entry point
description: Why this exists: developers and testers need a simple command to run the native client. What needs to be done: add packaging metadata or update existing Python package configuration, provide a console entry point such as lichen-tui, define dependency extras for BLE and CoAP if needed, document macOS/Linux prerequisites, and make local development invocation straightforward. Acceptance criteria: client launches from an installed console script; dependency groups are clear; import paths follow existing Python project layout; macOS and Linux setup notes cover BLE permissions or BlueZ requirements; packaging tests or smoke invocation are added.
notes:
Implementation evidence for Python TUI packaging/developer entry point:
- Added console alias lichen-native-client -> lichen.tui.native:main while preserving lichen-native-tui and existing simulator lichen-tui.
- Added explicit coap and native-client optional extras and refreshed uv.lock metadata; BLE remains a separate optional extra.
- Documented simulator TUI vs native LCI client commands, editable install examples, macOS Bluetooth permission note, Linux BlueZ/adapter/D-Bus requirements, and that automated tests must remain no-hardware by default.
- Added packaging tests that parse pyproject.toml for console scripts/extras and smoke native.main(["--help"]) without launching Textual or touching hardware.

Local validation:
- cd python && uv run ruff check src/lichen/tui tests/tui tests/test_packaging.py pyproject.toml -> passed.
- cd python && uv run mypy src/lichen/tui tests/tui tests/test_packaging.py -> passed, no issues in 5 source files.
- cd python && uv run pytest tests/tui tests/client tests/test_packaging.py -q -> 77 passed in 8.35s.
- git diff --check -> passed.

Code review:
- Fermat review: no P0/P1/P2 findings; script alias, extras metadata, lockfile, README notes, and smoke tests match acceptance.
- Bernoulli review: no blocking P0/P1/P2 findings; package metadata, aliases, extras/lockfile shape, no-hardware smoke test, and platform notes reviewed.

No EC2 used for this Python-only task.
